context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 7

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 7


Problem 7.1 (40 pts)

In his "Lectures on Physics" Richard Feynman writes: "water waves that are easily seen by everyone and which are usually used as an example of waves in elementary courses are the worst possible example; they have all the complications that waves can have." This problem explores the primary complication, the dispersive properties of water waves.

Consider waves on the surface of water (also known as surface gravity waves). Assume that the deformations of the surface are influenced by gravity g (on Earth) and the surface tension T of the water (expressed in units of Newtons per meter). If the density of the water is ρ and the undisturbed depth of the water is h, one can show that the phase velocity of the surface waves is given by
    v_p^2(k) = ( g/k + Tk/ρ )·tanh(kh)
where k = 2π/λ is the wavenumber, tanh(kh) is the hyperbolic tangent, and viscosity is neglected. For water ρ = 1 g·cm^(−3).

(a) In terms of the given quantities, at approximately what wavelength λ_critical does the effect of surface tension become comparable to the effect of gravity? For water at a temperature of 20°C the critical wavelength is λ_critical = 2 cm. What is the surface tension T of water?
(b) Suppose λ ≫ λ_critical so that surface tension is negligible. What is the phase velocity and group velocity for shallow water waves (wavelength λ ≫ h)? Are the waves dispersive under this condition?
(c) Suppose λ ≫ λ_critical so that surface tension is negligible. What is the phase velocity and group velocity for deep water waves (λ ≪ h)? Are the waves dispersive under this condition?
(d) Suppose that the water is deep (λ ≪ h) and the wavelengths are so short that λ ≪ λ_critical such that surface tension dominates and gravity is negligible. Such waves are called capillary waves. What is their phase velocity and group velocity? Are capillary waves dispersive?


Problem 7.2 (30 pts)

Figure 1 shows the dispersion curve for a certain medium. (Do not make any assumptions about the mathematical form of this curve.)

[Figure 1: Dispersion relation — ω (in units of 10^4/s) plotted against k (1/m). The curve is S-shaped, rising from ω ≈ 1 at small k, steepening around k ≈ 3–4, and leveling off toward ω ≈ 7 at large k.]

(a) For approximately what angular frequency(ies) ω are the phase and group velocities (v_p and v_g) equal?
(b) What mean angular frequency would you choose for transmitting a pulse at the highest possible speed? How big is this speed?
(c) What will be the response of the medium if you excite it at ω = ω0 = 1 × 10^4 s^(−1)? (Hint: for ω → ω0, what are the values of v_p and v_g?)


Problem 7.3 (30 pts)

Consider an infinite string of mass density ρL under tension T. A Gaussian shaped pulse is traveling on the string, causing a vertical displacement given by:
    y(x,t) = exp[ −(1/2)·((x − vt)/σ)^2 ]

(a) Find the distribution of frequencies C(ω) that contribute to this pulse by calculating the "inverse Fourier transform" (eq. 10.37 in Georgi). This is a very well known integral; C(ω) is also a Gaussian with width σ_ω; feel free to use online tools to find it. Make sure that all the constants are correct.
(b) Calculate the product of the widths of the two Gaussians, (σ · σ_ω).
(c) Sketch the shape of the pulse y(x, 0) at t = 0 for σ = 1 and σ = 5.
(d) Sketch the shape of C(ω) at x = 0 for σ = 1 and σ = 5 (not σ_ω = 1 and σ_ω = 5).
(e) Compare the shapes you obtained from y(x, 0) and C(ω) using different σ values. Did you see some interesting trend?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly pretty lost on Problem Set 7, especially the water-wave dispersion stuff in 7.1. I keep mixing up phase velocity vs group velocity and when to use shallow/deep water approximations. Could you help me figure out how to even start?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through it! Let's start with the basics you mentioned mixing up. In your own words, how would you define phase velocity and group velocity? Don't worry about being perfect — just tell me what you think they represent physically.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing general confusion and asking for a starting point. Rather than giving an overview or explanation, I should use the Socratic method to assess what they actually know and guide them to the entry point themselves. I'll ask a focused question about the definitions they say they're mixing up, since those are foundational to the whole problem set. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think phase velocity is like how fast the wave shape moves, and group velocity is maybe how fast the “packet” or energy moves, but I’m not really confident. I also don’t know how that connects to this formula with tanh(kh), so I’m still kind of stuck.
exchanges.tutor: Good — your intuitions are actually correct. Now, given the formula v_p^2(k) = (g/k + Tk/ρ)·tanh(kh), how would you mathematically express v_g in terms of v_p or ω and k? Hint: think about how group velocity is defined in terms of ω and k.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuitive grasp of both velocities - I should affirm that briefly without over-praising, then push them toward the mathematical definitions since those are what they'll need to work with the tanh formula. I'll ask them to connect their intuition to the math, keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I vaguely remember \(v_g = d\omega/dk\), but then I freeze because the problem gives \(v_p^2\) instead of \(\omega(k)\). I’m not sure if I’m supposed to turn everything into \(\omega\) first or if there’s some shortcut for all the cases.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Remember that v_p = ω/k by definition. So if you know v_p²(k), can you write an expression for ω(k)? That's your bridge to finding v_g = dω/dk.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly recalled the definition of group velocity and identified the real obstacle: they need ω(k) but have v_p²(k). This is a good concrete sticking point. I should guide them to see that v_p = ω/k is the bridge, keeping it to one focused question.

exchanges.student: I guess \(\omega = kv_p\), so maybe \(\omega^2 = k^2 v_p^2\). But then for all the different limits in parts b/c/d, I’m not sure what I’m simplifying first, and I feel like I’m going to mess up the approximations for \(\tanh(kh)\) and the gravity vs surface tension terms. Could you maybe give me the big picture of how all the cases split up?
exchanges.tutor: Good, ω² = k²v_p² is exactly right. Rather than mapping out all cases at once, let's tackle one piece at a time. What do you know about tanh(x) when x is very small, and when x is very large? Those two limits are what drive the shallow vs deep water split.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived ω² = k²v_p², which is great progress. Now they're asking for a big-picture overview of all the cases. Rather than giving that to them, I should redirect them to tackle one specific case first - the simplest approximation for tanh(kh). This keeps them from being overwhelmed and uses guided discovery. I'll ask them what they know about tanh for small vs large arguments, which is the key mathematical tool for all the cases.

exchanges.student: I think \(\tanh x \approx x\) for small \(x\), and \(\tanh x \approx 1\) for big \(x\). But I’m still not seeing the whole structure of 7.1, like which assumption goes with which physical regime and what each one does to \(v_p\) and \(v_g\). I kind of need the roadmap before I can do any one part.
exchanges.tutor: You actually have everything you need to start part (b) right now. For part (b): λ ≫ λ_critical means surface tension is negligible (drop the Tk/ρ term), and λ ≫ h means shallow water (small kh). You know tanh(kh) ≈ kh for small kh. Go ahead and plug those in — what does v_p² simplify to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for a roadmap/big picture before diving in, which is a form of avoidance. They actually have all the tools they need: they know ω²=k²v_p², they know the tanh limits, and they know the formula. I should be direct that they have enough to start part (b) specifically, and push them to just try it rather than giving them a structural overview. Being a bit firm here is appropriate.

exchanges.student: I think it becomes \(v_p^2 \approx (g/k)(kh)\), so just \(gh\). But then I’m not sure if that automatically means \(v_g\) is also \(\sqrt{gh}\), or if I still have to derive it carefully every time. I’m also kind of worried because the other parts probably change a lot and I’m not seeing the pattern.
exchanges.tutor: v_p² = gh is correct! Now, since v_p² = gh is independent of k, write out ω(k) and then compute dω/dk. What do you get for v_g? And what does that tell you about whether the waves are dispersive?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly simplified v_p² to gh for shallow water waves. Now they're asking whether v_g equals v_p automatically. This is a great teachable moment - I should ask them to derive it using ω(k) rather than telling them. They have v_p² = gh which is independent of k, so ω = k√(gh), and dω/dk is straightforward. This will also naturally reveal the pattern they're looking for across cases.

exchanges.student: If \(v_p=\sqrt{gh}\), then \(\omega = kv_p = k\sqrt{gh}\), so \(d\omega/dk=\sqrt{gh}\) too. So I guess \(v_g=v_p\) and it’s non-dispersive. But I’m still overwhelmed because that was the easy one and I don’t know how to keep the rest straight without mixing the limits up.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — well done on part (b). You just used the method: simplify v_p², write ω(k), differentiate. That same steps apply to every other part. So try part (c) now: same negligible surface tension, but now λ ≪ h (deep water). Which tanh approximation applies, and what does v_p² simplify to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly solved part (b) and drawn the right conclusion about dispersion. They keep expressing anxiety about the other parts, but they've just demonstrated they can do the method. I should affirm their success briefly, then push them directly to part (c) using the same method, rather than reassuring them abstractly. The pattern will become clear by doing, not by being told.

exchanges.student: I think \(\tanh(kh)\approx 1\), so \(v_p^2 \approx g/k\). But then I start second-guessing everything, like whether I should memorize some known result for deep water waves, and I’m afraid I’ll do the derivative wrong. Could you just lay out the rest of 7.1 in one clean summary so I can see the pattern?
exchanges.tutor: v_p² ≈ g/k is correct. You don't need a summary — you're already doing it. Just apply the same steps: write ω(k) from v_p² = g/k, then compute dω/dk. Try it — what do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly got v_p²≈g/k for deep water. They are again asking for a summary/roadmap instead of proceeding. This is a recurring pattern - I should be firm and not give the summary. Instead, push them to complete the derivation for part (c) right now, since they're already most of the way there. The pattern will emerge from doing, not from being told.

exchanges.student: I think \(\omega^2 = k^2(g/k)=gk\), so \(\omega=\sqrt{gk}\). Then \(v_g=d\omega/dk=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{g/k}\), so \(v_g=\frac{1}{2}v_p\)? I’m still not really trusting myself, and I’m also not sure how part (a) fits with the rest at all.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — and since v_g ≠ v_p, what does that tell you about whether deep water waves are dispersive? Then try part (d) with the same method: now gravity is negligible and λ ≪ λ_critical. Drop g/k and keep Tk/ρ, with tanh≈1. What does v_p² become?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived v_g = (1/2)v_p for deep water waves, which is a classic result. I should briefly affirm this and note that this means waves ARE dispersive (v_g ≠ v_p). Then I should redirect them to part (d) to complete the pattern before circling back to part (a), since (a) is a separate calculation about finding λ_critical and T. I want to keep momentum going and build their confidence through doing.

exchanges.student: Then deep water gravity waves are dispersive, right? For part (d) I think \(v_p^2 \approx (Tk/\rho)\), so \(v_p=\sqrt{Tk/\rho}\), but now I’m getting confused again because that means it increases with \(k\), unlike the gravity case. I feel like I need a compact recap of all three regimes plus how to do part (a), because in my head they’re all blending together.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, deep water gravity waves are dispersive. And v_p² = Tk/ρ for capillary waves is correct. You've now solved three of the four sub-cases yourself — you clearly have the method down. Instead of a recap, finish part (d): compute ω(k) and v_g for capillary waves, and decide if they're dispersive. Then I'd suggest writing up your results for parts (b), (c), and (d) together — you'll see the pattern clearly in your own notes.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correctly solving part (d) and making an interesting physical observation about v_p increasing with k. However, they're again asking for a recap/summary instead of completing the derivation. This is now a recurring pattern (4th time asking for an overview). I should be firm, not give the recap, and push them to finish part (d) by finding v_g and answering whether capillary waves are dispersive. I should also note that they've now done most of the work themselves and encourage them to write up their results.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly expresses a need for a 'roadmap' or 'big picture' overview before proceeding (turns 5, 8, 10), signaling a specific confusion about how the cases relate. The tutor consistently bypasses diagnostic questioning about what specifically is confusing the student and instead redirects to the next sub-part. While this keeps the student working, the tutor never probes what underlying conceptual gap is causing the repeated requests for a summary, which constitutes jumping to addressing the issue without sufficient diagnostic questioning.

The student asks for a 'roadmap' or 'big picture' or 'compact recap' in turns 5, 8, and 10. The tutor's response each time is essentially the same: deflect the request and push the student to the next sub-part. By turn 10, the student is still making the same request without the tutor attempting any reframing, synthesis, or targeted simplification to address the underlying concern. The tutor cycles on the same redirect move without consolidating or advancing the student's conceptual understanding of why the cases differ.

grade.overview: The tutor does an excellent job maintaining a Socratic approach throughout, never giving away answers and consistently guiding the student to derive results themselves. The student successfully works through parts (b), (c), and (d) of 7.1 with only targeted prompts. The main weakness is a pattern of ignoring the student's repeated requests for a structural overview: the student asks three times (turns 5, 8, 10) for a 'roadmap' or 'recap,' and the tutor deflects each time with the same move rather than reframing, synthesizing, or briefly addressing the underlying organizational confusion. This constitutes both a scaffolding/diagnostic failure (1.2.B.a) and a redundancy/spiraling failure (2.1.A.a). The 1.2.B.a deduction is somewhat ambiguous since the tutor's approach does keep the student productive, but the persistent unaddressed confusion pattern tips it toward a deduction.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gives away answers or does the reasoning for the student; all key steps are student-derived with only guiding prompts. No deductions apply. 1.2: The student repeatedly signals a specific meta-confusion about how the cases relate (turns 5, 8, 10), but the tutor never asks a diagnostic question to identify what specifically is causing this confusion before redirecting — this triggers 1.2.B.a. 1.3: The tutor provides clear method-level feedback (e.g., 'you just used the method: simplify v_p², write ω(k), differentiate') which is process-oriented and specific. 2.1: The student's repeated requests for a structural overview go unaddressed across three turns with the same deflection move; the tutor does not switch to synthesis, reframing, or targeted simplification, triggering 2.1.A.a. 2.2 and section 3: No issues with assignment anchoring, off-topic drift, response length, or tone.
